channel.title: NASA 新闻 RSS（简体中文版）
channel.description: NASA 官方新闻 + 英译简

item.title: NASA 公布 2026 年人类着陆器挑战赛获奖者
item.description:
NASA 宣布了 2026 年人类登陆器挑战赛参与者为未来载人月球登陆器的环境控制和生命支持系统开发的顶尖学生解决方案。这一宣布标志着大学团队数月研究的成果，这些团队致力于推进支持该机构阿耳忒弥斯计划的技术，该计划将于 2028 年让美国宇航员重返月球。
此次挑战赛于 6 月 25 日在阿拉巴马州亨茨维尔的 NASA 马歇尔太空飞行中心附近进行了最终技术演示后结束。自 2025 年 9 月以来，来自全国各地的学生团队设计了系统级方法，以提高宇航员在深空任务期间至关重要的环境控制和生命支持技术的性能和可靠性。
NASA 马歇尔任务能力和风险降低高级能力集成负责人 Natalie Martinez-Vlasoff 表示：“随着 NASA 继续为持续的月球探索和未来人类火星任务做准备，开发强大、高效和可靠的生命支持系统仍然是一个关键的重点领域。” “2026 届学生团队表现出对这些系统的设计选择范围的深刻理解，以及深思熟虑的系统级方法如何能够提高使用未来人类着陆系统的宇航员的可靠性和机组人员安全。看到学生们贡献有助于使长期月球探索更加容易实现的想法，这是令人鼓舞的。”
6 月 22 日，入围团队齐聚亨茨维尔的美国太空与火箭中心，在合作海报会议上向 NASA 和航空航天业专家组成的小组以及同行展示他们的研究成果。年度竞赛以颁奖典礼结束，表彰 12 支决赛入围队伍中表现最好的队伍。
NASA 宣布加州理工州立大学因其基于 Peltier 的水合积累终端项目成为总冠军并获得 10,000 美元的最高奖金。普渡大学因其增强型饮用水分配器的工作获得第二名并获得 5,000 美元的奖金，其次是代托纳比奇的安柏里德尔航空大学，因其先进质量轨道补水组装项目获得 3,000 美元的奖金获得第三名。
人类着陆器挑战赛旨在激励和吸引下一代工程师和科学家，因为美国宇航局及其合作伙伴准备将宇航员送上月球，为未来的火星任务做准备。载人着陆系统是将宇航员带到月球表面并在阿耳忒弥斯下返回月球轨道的运输方式。
通过人类着陆器挑战赛等竞赛，美国宇航局培养了下一代工程师和研究人员，同时推进宇航员探索深空所需的技术。这些举措支持该机构的探索目标，同时培养未来航空航天专业人士的实践能力、解决问题能力和系统思维能力。人类着陆器挑战赛的学生解决方案可以纳入下一代阿耳忒弥斯着陆器的当前工作中。
由美国宇航局马歇尔管理的美国宇航局载人着陆系统计划赞助了这项由国家航空航天研究所管理的挑战。
通过阿尔忒弥斯计划，美国宇航局将派遣宇航员探索月球，以实现科学发现、经济利益，并为首次载人火星任务奠定基础——造福所有人。
有关阿耳忒弥斯计划的更多信息，请访问：

item.title: 美国宇航局测试用于未来太空加油任务的新型加油装置
item.description:
要观看此视频，请启用 JavaScript，并考虑升级到支持 HTML5 视频的网络浏览器
对于美国宇航局的下一代深空探索任务，航天器可能需要在地球轨道上补充燃料，然后再进一步推进太阳系。类似于气泵需要喷嘴来安装燃料箱，未来的航天器可能需要一种特殊的装置才能在出发前加注，称为低温耦合器。
低温耦合器将使航天器能够连接到未来的轨道推进剂库，该库将充当太空加油站。该技术面临着可靠传输低温或超冷流体而不损失推进剂或性能的挑战。液氢和液氧等低温推进剂必须保持在零华氏度以下数百度的温度，这对移动它们的材料、密封件和机制提出了严格的要求。
位于阿拉巴马州亨茨维尔的 NASA 马歇尔太空飞行中心低温耦合器项目经理特拉维斯·贝尔彻 (Travis Belcher) 表示：“两个航天器之间的在轨低温加油尚未完成，这仍然是航天中最艰巨的工程挑战之一。” “这些推进剂传输对于 NASA 未来想要执行的任务至关重要，因此开发一种能够处理超冷推进剂的耦合器是实现这种能力的关键一步。”
地面耦合器（例如用于填充阿耳忒弥斯任务的 SLS（太空发射系统）的耦合器）不适用于轨道推进剂传输。这些耦合器在火箭发射时会快速释放，并且必须在下次飞行时手动重新连接。它们也不是为在恶劣的太空环境中运行而设计的，而且比用于补充轨道航天器燃料箱的燃料大得多。
为了应对这些挑战，NASA 测试了 L3Harris 开发的低温耦合器。
“我们正在研究的低温耦合器可以多次连接和分离，并且是完全自动化的，因此宇航员不必进行太空行走来转移推进剂，”贝尔彻说。 “它们经过严格设计，可以承受预期的储罐设计所需的空间和尺寸。”
NASA 和 L3Harris 联合团队最近在 NASA 马歇尔进行了两种类型的测试。为了确保低温耦合器能够应对其所面临的极冷温度，他们通过多个连接和断开配置运行 -321 华氏度的液氮，以观察耦合器对热收缩、流动以及推进剂和材料之间的显着温差的反应。
该团队还对低温耦合器进行了操作测试，以确定其性能极限。在此设置中，耦合器的一半安装在可以沿任何方向移动和旋转的机器人工作台上，使其能够模拟与另一半未对准的对接，另一半在工作台上方保持静止。低温耦合器的设计可以适应一些未对准的情况，以防航天器和基地在对接时未完全对准。
“这些低温耦合器处于开发早期，因此测试主要集中在基本功能上，”贝尔彻说。 “未来的测试活动将为特定任务设计它们，并根据该任务的要求更仔细地评估它们。”
低温耦合器测试是作为 2022 年合作机会公告的一部分进行的，NASA 中心在该合作伙伴关系中为精选公司免费提供专业知识、设施、硬件和软件。
低温流体管理组合项目是一个位于美国宇航局马歇尔和美国宇航局克利夫兰格伦研究中心的跨机构团队，负责监督低温耦合器的开发。
要了解有关低温流体管理的更多信息，请访问：

item.title: NASA 合作伙伴为 6 月启动 Swift Boost 任务做好准备
item.description:
A mission to raise the orbit of NASA’s Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory is poised for launch no earlier than Tuesday, June 30, 6:23 a.m. EDT (10:23 p.m. UTC+12), from Kwajalein Atoll, part of the Republic of the Marshall Islands in the South Pacific Ocean.
A robotic servicing satellite called LINK, built by Katalyst Space, will blast into orbit on aNorthrop Grumman Pegasus XL rocket. LINK will rendezvous with, grapple, and slowly raise Swift’s altitude over several months, preventing it from re-entering Earth’s atmosphere later this year.
“Swift is NASA’s multitool when it comes to studying the cosmos,” said S. Bradley Cenko, principal investigator,Swift,NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Centerin Greenbelt, Maryland. “It observes the sky using a wide range of light and rapidly points at short-lived outbursts, alerting other facilities in space and on the ground to help coordinate follow-up observations. For the last two decades, Swift has been a key player in NASA’s efforts to understand how the universe works, and we’re looking forward to getting back to that work after the boost is complete.”
Our planet’s atmosphere creates drag on all spacecraft in low Earth orbit, gradually reducing their altitudes if they don’t have propulsion systems to counteract the effect.
Arecent boutof increased solar activity magnified this impact on Swift, whichlaunchedin November 2004.
Rather than allowing Swift to re-enter the atmosphere as many missions do, NASA is using the opportunity to advance the U.S. commercial satellite servicing industry.
In September, the agencycontractedKatalyst to attempt to boost the observatory. The company would have less than one year to design, build, test, and launch a satellite to meet, grab, and lift Swift to nearly its original orbit.
"Swift wasn't designed to be serviced," said Ghonhee Lee, CEO of Katalyst. "By demonstrating we can quickly and cost-effectively extend its lifetime, we're creating a blueprint for servicing spacecraft that were never designed for on-orbit maintenance. If we're going to build an enduring presence beyond Earth, we need the capability to manipulate our environment in space. That means deploying robotic spacecraft that can reposition, repair, refuel, and refit satellites after launch.”
The LINK spacecraft weighs about 880 pounds and stands about 5 feet tall, about a third of Swift’s overall size. Nearly 20 feet of solar panels will power three ion thrusters and a trio of robotic arms.
LINK completedenvironmental testingthat mimicked launch and space-like conditions at NASA Goddard this spring, as well as additional preflight assessments at Katalyst’s facility in Broomfield, Colorado.
For the boost to have its best chance of success, Swift needs to stay above an altitude of about 185 miles.
By the end of last year, however,orbital predictionsgenerated by NASA showed the observatory reaching that threshold as early as July.
To slow Swift’s descent, theoperations teamat Penn State’s Eberly College of Science altered how they managed and oriented the spacecraft.
Unlike during normal operating procedures, where Swift looks at spots on the sky that are scientifically interesting, the team now selects targets that steer Swift into the most streamlined position. They also reduced power consumption as much as possible to place the satellite’s large solar panels in a more aerodynamic orientation.
Recent orbital predictions show these changes will keep Swift above critical altitude until this fall.
The satellite will launch aboard the Pegasus XL.
“We can deploy Pegasus from almost anywhere in the world using ourStargazer, a modified L-1011 aircraft,” said Wes Collier, vice president of launch systems at Northrop Grumman. “That combination of flexibility and responsive access to space will help LINK quickly reach Swift, giving the teams time to complete the boost.”
Earlier this month, engineers loaded LINK into the Pegasus XL and attached the rocket to Stargazer atNASA’s Wallops Flight Facilityin Virginia. The aircraft and its payloaddepartedfor Kwajalein Atoll on Thursday, June 18, where it now awaits launch.
Once in orbit, LINK will undergo several weeks of commissioning as Katalyst evaluates the spacecraft’s propulsion, navigation, and sensor systems. It then will slowly approach and survey Swift before grabbing the observatory with its robotic arms and slowly raising the orbit to nearly 370 miles.
“This is a high-risk, high-reward mission,” said Shawn Domagal-Goldman, division director, Astrophysics,NASA Headquartersin Washington. “Swift plays a notable role in our fleet. We have much to gain by attempting this boost, which is more affordable than trying to replace Swift’s capabilities and allows NASA to advance the nation’s satellite servicing industry, for the benefit of all.”
https://science.nasa.gov/mission/swift/swift-boost-mission/
ByJeanette KazmierczakGoddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.
Media contacts:Alise FisherHeadquarters, Washington20

item.title: NASA 确定了 40 多项空间技术进行合作
item.description:
NASA 从 37 家公司中选出了 41 项提案，以推进技术发展，以支持该机构的目标，即在月球上建立长期存在并实现人类探索火星。
这些美国公司是从 NASA 2025 年合作机会公告 (ACO) 中挑选出来的，它们将成熟技术，为太空运输、行星表面操作和月球表面基础设施创建解决方案。
美国宇航局华盛顿总部研究与技术任务理事会高级研究与技术部主任格雷格·斯托弗表示：“我们正在帮助美国工业界成为美国宇航局月球、火星及其他太空任务的积极合作伙伴。” “通过利用商业产业，美国宇航局可以快速发展关键能力来支持其最雄心勃勃的任务，同时培育国家强劲的太空经济。”
NASA 的 ACO 在机构和行业之间建立了互惠互利的合作伙伴关系，而无需进行资金交换。通过这个机会，公司可以利用 NASA 的专业设施、软件、硬件和主题专家，使他们能够快速成熟其技术，以适应商业市场和未来的政府任务。
自 2015 年启动第一个 ACO 以来，NASA 已支持了 110 多个项目。支持这些协议的机构资源总价值约为 3000 万美元，其中还利用了 3200 万美元的行业捐款。每份协议的履行期限将协商确定，预计期限为12至24个月。
行业提案者的任务是响应机构技术主题，这些主题将受益于公私伙伴关系的快速发展，包括太空运输发动机元件、制导和导航系统、着陆系统、空间服务组装和制造以及能源管理技术。
完整的选择列表可以在该机构的网站上找到，并且涵盖跨领域功能，包括：
洛克希德·马丁公司将成熟一种模块化、紧凑的能源解决方案，可以支持月球永久阴影区域的持续发电，帮助未来的船员和资源度过漫长的月夜。该公司的无线功率传输系统旨在利用光纤激光器和天基排热系统来推进功率束技术，以提高耐用性。
为了加强轨道任务，Kall Morris Inc. 将开发 Asteria，一种补充有效载荷附件系统。 Asteria 可以使用非破坏性控释粘合剂附着到传统、当前和下一代轨道资产上，无需预先安装基础设施。该技术可实现先进的机动、改进的物体跟踪、资产保护、数据收集和卫星寿命延长。
Moonprint Solutions 是一家小型企业，提出了灵活的隔离盖，以保护关键硬件和系统免受恶劣月球环境中磨料粉尘的影响。柔性外壳可以为各种硬件提供符合复杂形状的保护，从而提供战略优势。这些耐用的盖子可用于漫游车、机器人关节、软管和其他铰接设备，以支持在月球和火星上的长期运行。
选定的项目可能会对商业航天领域产生重大影响，例如扩大现有市场或开辟新市场、降低价格、增加选择或提供全新的功能。
有兴趣与 NASA 一起开发空间技术的组织可以在线探索机会。
有关 NASA 太空技术投资的更多信息，请访问：
Jennifer Dooren / Rob Margetta华盛顿总部202-358-1600jennifer.m.dooren@nasa.gov/robert.j.margetta@nasa.gov

item.title: 欧几里得看到银河系的心脏
item.description:
欧洲航天局 (ESA) 的欧几里得任务 (Euclid) 与 NASA 共同参与，对银河系的中心进行了新的观察，如 2026 年 6 月 24 日发布的这张图像所示。这次观测与科学家将用 NASA 的南希·格雷斯·罗马太空望远镜观测的区域重叠，该望远镜将于今年夏天晚些时候发射。这一先睹为快为天文学家的罗马核心调查提供了一个重大的启动机会，帮助科学家们比单独使用任何一台望远镜都能了解更多的信息。
阅读更多关于欧几里得和罗曼将看到的内容。
图片来源：ESA/Euclid/Euclid Consortium/NASA、CFHT，图像处理由 J.-C. Cuillandre 和 E. Bertin（CEA 巴黎萨克雷）

item.title: 向 NASA 传送信号
item.description:
Growing up on the central California coast, watching rocket launches with his father was part of Eric Fernandez’s childhood routine. Fernandez had posters of rockets on the wall, but despite being fascinated by them, he never imagined one day this would be his career. Because both of his grandparents had served at Vandenberg Air Force Base (later renamed to Vandenberg Space Force Base), he assumed that the launches from there were for the military. NASA didn’t cross his mind. The space agency seemed very far away from a place like Orcutt, California, a small town situated among rolling hills covered with farms and vineyards.
Fernandez had been part of a painting crew for several years after high school. While it paid the rent, it wasn’t what he wanted to do with his life. However, he found something he enjoyed. He had started at his future father-in-law’s appliance store, working as a technician, repairing and installing appliances. He excelled at the work and planned to stay there with the goal to eventually run the small business.
It was from a friend about an opening for something called telemetry. Fernandez wasn’t sure what that meant. He was happy with his current career path. He nearly declined the offer, but after some persuading, he decided to go for the interview at a NASA building on the military base.
“I walked in the telemetry lab, and I see oscilloscopes, screens with squiggly lines, lots of blinking lights, and things I didn’t know about at the time,” Fernandez recounted. “I was very curious about it, so I was asking a million questions as we toured the lab, and they were asking about me. They really liked my background, especially my electronics experience, my troubleshooting skills, and my ability to solder.”
He received an offer for a technician position from a company that provided support to NASA under the Expendable Launch Vehicle Integrated Support, or ELVIS, contract. Fernandez had to make an important decision about his future.
“I prayed about it and met with my father-in-law,” said Fernandez. “I decided to change career paths and start a new career as a contractor working with NASA, supporting its Launch Services Program.”
That was 17 years ago, and he has been working there ever since, advancing to telemetry engineer in 2019. He has contributed to 27 launches for NASA, supporting scientific and robotic exploration missions. He’s also supported hundreds of launches for the U.S. military and commercial sector, as part of the agency’s efforts to work with its partners to understand the capabilities of the commercial rocket fleet.
While Fernandez wasn’t planning on making additional changes, a new opportunity presented itself earlier this year. The agency decided to strengthen its core capabilities by bringing mission-critical positions into the civil service.
When he had the opportunity to join the civil service at NASA, Fernandez applied. On June 15, he swore in at Vandenberg bringing his knowledge and experience to the agency, ready to become an official part of a group he already considered family.
“Telemetry is the collection of remote measurements that let us know the rocket is healthy when it’s fueling on the pad, when it’s in flight, and when it’s placing a spacecraft into the proper orbit,” said Fernandez. “It’s our job to make sure decision makers have all the right data to make the right calls in real time. We can’t afford to give them bad data.”
Fernandez’s team has multiple ways of getting the data when a rocket is on the launch pad, including ground data streams and radio frequencies link. Each data path is carefully tested beforehand using tools like bit-error-rate tests, called BERTs, that send pseudo-random patterns to help determine the health of the networks. Once the data is received, the team verifies it using frame sync patterns and word counters, sequenced data embedded in the stream. During ascent, they rely on ground tracking stations and dedicated satellites to relay data. All of it is recorded for posterity and post-flight review. The entire process requires extensive planning, coordination, and constant learning as the industry continues to innovate.
“You’re going to be humbled because the technology is always moving forward, and a new challenge is going to arise,” Fernandez said. “But there’s nothing we haven’t conquered, and there’s not a problem we haven’t figured out yet.”
He credits his teammates. He described his team as “iron sharpening iron.”
Today, Fernandez still lives in Orcutt, seven houses down from where he grew up. His children go to the same schools and play in the same parks he did. He still watches rocket launches, but now he does it with his children when he’s not supporting a launch for the agency.
While he spends his days at work looking ahead to the future, as part of a team that explores the Moon, Mars, and beyond, he hasn’t forgotten where he came from.
“I just wish I could go back and tell little boy Eric, you’re going

item.title: NASA 的 PACE 任务研究烟雾和火灾
item.description:
随着北美火灾季节的到来，全国范围内烧毁的英亩数已创历史新高，美国宇航局的浮游生物、气溶胶、云和海洋生态系统 (PACE) 卫星的三台仪器正在观测火灾前兆的植被以及烟雾及其运动。这些数据将帮助科学家拼凑线索，加深对野火的了解。
“我们面临的挑战是获取这些线索并以有意义的方式使用它们，以便我们的地球模型正确地代表正在发生的事情，”在马里兰州格林贝尔特 NASA 戈达德太空飞行中心从事 PACE 任务的遥感科学家 Kirk Knobelspiesse 说。
虽然这颗卫星于 2024 年 2 月发射，旨在研究地球的海洋和大气层，但它具有意想不到的功能：监测植被变化。它还可以告诉我们烧伤疤痕，即野火后留下的烧焦的土地区域。
“PACE 卫星也可以观测陆地，而且效果非常好，”美国宇航局戈达德空间站 PACE 任务地面负责人斯凯·卡普兰 (Skye Caplan) 说道。 “新的高光谱数据集有很多东西值得探索。”
PACE 上的海洋颜色仪器是一种高光谱仪器，可以用数百个不同波长的可见光、近红外和紫外光来观察地球。这种广泛的光谱使其能够收集有关植物健康状况的数据，例如植物的胁迫状态、干燥度以及相对色素平衡，所有这些都有助于识别高火灾风险区域。土地管理者可以使用这些数据来分配资源，以帮助减轻火灾风险。
该仪器每天观察整个地球，在高纬度地区覆盖更频繁。通过这个频率，在晴朗的日子里，PACE 科学家可以快速评估火灾的后果，确定烧伤疤痕的位置和跨度。被野火烧毁的地区经常会面临更大的洪水和山体滑坡风险。卡普兰说，识别这些高风险领域并监控它们随时间的演变非常重要。
利用紫外线范围内的波长，海洋颜色仪器还可以监测火灾后的烟雾，以及有关这些颗粒在大气中漂移多高的信息——高度在颗粒移动的距离及其影响的系统中发挥着重要作用。该仪器以其紫外线数据扩展了其他卫星仪器的火灾观测，例如可见红外成像辐射计套件和中分辨率成像光谱辐射计。
美国宇航局戈达德海洋颜色仪器 PACE 大气项目科学负责人安德鲁·塞耶 (Andrew Sayer) 表示，PACE 上的另外两台仪器，即超角度彩虹旋光仪 2 和行星探索分光旋光仪 1 号，富含有关来自不同地区的气溶胶成分的信息。
通过测量大气中颗粒反射的光特性，这两种仪器可以确定这些颗粒的数量，以及它们的化学性质、颜色、大小和形状。科学家利用这些信息来区分烟雾和其他颗粒物。烟雾颗粒通常具有吸光性（呈灰色、黑色或棕色），并且与 PACE 视图中的其他气溶胶（例如污染物和灰尘）相比，其尺寸较小。
卫星旋光仪负责人 Knobelspiesse 表示，PACE 的数据将帮助科学家创建更准确的野火模型并模拟未来事件。 “我们将能够研究未来不同的排放情况，并了解一个地点产生的烟雾如何影响地球系统的其他部分。”
美国宇航局戈达德太空飞行中心，马里兰州格林贝尔特
地球的森林和植被一年内可能会发生很多变化，比如春天和……
光和光学的一些相同特性使天空呈蓝色……
在过去的十年中，野火加剧了邻近大部分地区的地面臭氧污染……

item.title: 哈勃间谍星光枝形吊灯
item.description:
今天美国宇航局/欧洲航天局哈勃太空望远镜图像的主题是我们银河系的古代居民。这个闪闪发光的场景以球状星团为特色：由数万到数百万颗恒星组成的集合，它们在重力的影响下紧密地结合在一起。我们的银河系中有超过 150 个球状星团，尽管可能还有其他球状星团尚未被发现，被尘埃或密集的恒星场隐藏起来。
这个球状星团 NGC 6723，有时被称为枝形吊灯星团，很像它的同名星团，因为它闪烁着无数的光芒。然而，这盏吊灯中的每个“灯泡”都是距离我们 27,000 光年的人马座（射手座）中的一颗恒星。
像 NGC 6723 这样的球状星团包含我们银河系中一些最古老的恒星。这些星团的年龄通常超过 100 亿年，有些星团的年龄几乎与宇宙本身一样古老。天文学家认为球状星团是我们银河系中最早形成的结构之一，它们可能比太阳绕其运行的薄薄恒星盘早数十亿年合并在一起。然而，球状星团如何形成的细节尚不清楚。
天文学家最初认为球状星团中的所有恒星都是在一次恒星形成过程中同时形成的。这意味着球状星团中的所有恒星都具有相同的年龄并且由相同的化学元素混合物组成。现在，借助哈勃等望远镜的观测，研究人员知道这些看似简单的恒星群体有着比最初想象的更复杂的历史。
哈勃首次观测 NGC 6723 是一项雄心勃勃的调查的一部分，该调查致力于揭开银河系球状星团特性的神秘面纱。在这个观测计划（#10775，PI：Sarajedini）中，研究人员使用哈勃望远镜以不可见光和近红外光研究了我们银河系中的 65 个球状星团。这些数据使研究人员能够研究从球状星团的年龄到大质量恒星沉入星团中心和低质量恒星漂向星团外围的过程等一切内容。这项调查具有巨大的科学价值，这些观察结果启发了数百篇已发表的研究论文。
在后来的观测计划（#13297，PI：Piotto）中，研究人员再次将目光投向许多相同的星团，包括 NGC 6723。这一次，他们利用哈勃对紫外线的独特敏感性来检测球状星团中恒星之间化学成分的细微变化，并确定星团中恒星的年龄分布。对于 NGC 6723，研究人员发现了两个间隔很近的恒星形成时期的证据，第二个时期发生在第一个时期的 6.34 亿年内。 （“间隔很近”是相对的；对于一个年龄超过 100 亿年的星团来说，6.34 亿年只是一眨眼的功夫！）
得益于这些发现，天文学家正在了解球状星团如何以及何时形成，而哈勃对 NGC 6723 等天体枝形吊灯的观测正在照亮道路。
克莱尔·安德烈奥利美国宇航局戈达德太空飞行中心，格林贝尔特，MDclaire.andreoli@nasa.gov
自 1990 年发射以来，哈勃太空望远镜改变了我们对宇宙的基本理解。

item.title: 沙利文岛之战
item.description:
1776 年 6 月 28 日，南卡罗来纳州查尔斯顿附近的障壁岛发生了一场关键战役，沼泽、沙地和无法通行的入口帮助殖民军队击退了英国军队。
1776 年 6 月 28 日，当托马斯·杰斐逊和五人委员会在费城提交《独立宣言》初稿时，几艘英国军舰和数千名士兵正在南卡罗来纳州的沙利文岛周围集结。
六月的一天，争夺查尔斯顿港口沙障岛的激烈战斗是独立战争早期阶段最重要的战斗之一。到了夜幕降临时，基本上未经考验的殖民军队决定性地击败了英国人，这一结果帮助查尔斯顿免遭占领，并在战争的关键阶段鼓舞了美国人的士气。
Landsat 8 卫星于 2026 年 6 月 3 日拍摄到了该岛的这张图像。 250 年前，该岛的沙滩、盐沼和总体形状看起来很相似，尽管道路或其他人类开发迹象的证据较少。
然而，岛上肯定有一些人类活动的迹象。相当引人注目的是沙利文堡，这是一座由棕榈原木建造的大型方形建筑，位于岛屿南端，靠近港口入口处。虽然堡垒的一侧主要由奴隶组装而成，在战斗时尚未完工，但另一侧却有 16 英尺宽的墙壁，里面填满了沙子，并包含安装了 31 门大炮的木板炮台。
历史地图显示，至少有一条道路从沙利文岛的南端延伸至北端，数百名殖民士兵也驻扎在那里，以保护裂口湾免遭附近长岛（现棕榈岛）集结的约 3,000 名英国军队的袭击。历史学家估计，战斗开始时，大约有 800 名殖民军队，其中包括数十名卡托巴战士，保卫着沙利文岛的东北部，嵌入土制防御工事中，并配备两门火炮。
1776 年 6 月 28 日上午，英国发动进攻时，军事战术和地理因素都在决定结果方面发挥了关键作用。在被告知退潮时入海口的水深不到 18 英寸（46 厘米）后，英国指挥官计划让他的部队步行穿过 Breach Inlet。但当他意识到退潮时最浅的部分至少有 7 英尺（2 米）深时，他被迫转向使用平底船进行更危险的两栖攻击。乘坐平底船旅行限制了可以同时穿越英吉利海峡的英国军队数量，使殖民地守军更容易在全天的激烈小冲突中击退他们。
在岛的另一边，英国军舰已在沙利文堡附近抛锚，并开始向该堡发射数千枚炮弹和爆炸弹。然而，棕榈木的天然耐用性和柔韧性像海绵一样吸收传入的火焰。
大多数落入堡垒墙内的炮弹都被抵消了。堡垒指挥官威廉·莫尔特里上校后来在回忆录中指出，堡垒中心有一片沼泽地“沼泽”，“立即”“吞噬”了来袭的火力。他写道，那些越过城墙并“落在堡垒内和​​周围的沙子里的炮弹立即被掩埋，因此很少有炮弹在我们中间爆炸”。
殖民者利用有限的火药，将火力集中在载有英国指挥官彼得·帕克爵士的船只上，严重损坏了船只，最终导致船上 40 人死亡。到了晚上，经过十个小时的战斗，英军已经筋疲力尽，而且进展甚微，他们撤退了。
“我们一生中从未经历过这样的惨败，”一位皇家海军水手写道。战斗结束后，这座堡垒被称为莫尔特里堡，棕榈树开始出现在州印章上，这被证明是殖民自豪感和抵抗的持久象征。战斗六天后，《独立宣言》在费城获得通过。
NASA 地球观测站图像由 Michala Garrison 使用来自美国的陆地卫星数据拍摄地质调查。亚当·沃伊兰的故事。
当我们探索宇宙并发现更多关于我们的家园星球时，请随时了解 NASA 的最新内容。
海狸岛是密歇根湖北部群岛上一串青翠美丽的宝石中的一个。
冰冷、与世隔绝的彼得一世岛在南极西海岸附近的大气层中掀起了一场表演。
大火蔓延到加州海峡群岛国家公园第二大岛的南侧。
订阅地球观测站并将地球放入您的收件箱中。
美国宇航局地球观测站每天为您带来地球的深入故事和令人惊叹的图像。
开放访问 NASA 的地球科学数据档案

item.title: Expedition 73 船员反思科学、团队合作和轨道生活
item.description:
Johnson Space Center Office of Communications
On June 16, astronauts and cosmonauts gathered at Space Center Houston to share stories from their missions aboard the International Space Station and recognize the teamwork and people on the ground that made their missions possible.
The Expedition 73 Welcome Home Ceremony brought together members of NASA’s SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27, and NASA’s SpaceX Crew-11 missions. During the event, the crews reflected on the science, partnerships, and international collaboration that defined their time in orbit.
Remarks were delivered by NASA’s Johnson Space Center Director Vanessa Wyche, Low Earth Orbit Program’s Deputy Manager for the International Space Station Dina Contella, Richard Jones with NASA’s commercial crew office, Flight Operations Director Norm Knight, Johnson Employee Relations Lead David Kelley, and Space Center Houston Chief Operating and Strategy Officer Keesha Bullock. Together, they recognized the accomplishments of the crews and the team members who helped make the expedition a success.
Wyche welcomed the crews home and reflected on the accomplishments of Expedition 73.
“Together, these crews exemplified professionalism, resilience, and the spirit of international cooperation,” Wyche said. “Their work ensured the continued success of the International Space Station Program and demonstrated the strength of our multi-vehicle crew transportation strategy.”
During the expedition, all available docking ports were occupied simultaneously for the first time, with eight spacecraft attached to the station. The crew also supported visiting missions, includingAxiom Mission 4, and multiple cargo deliveries while maintaining a full schedule of scientific investigations.
Crew members completed three spacewalks, installing hardware that supports future solar array upgrades and maintenance activities critical to station operations.
The ceremony also recognized the workforce whose dedication supported every aspect of Expedition 73, from mission planning and operations to research, training, and crew safety.
“You learned each other’s languages, and often, when we didn’t know the right answers, you partnered with us, and you would come up with the answers and help,” said International Space Station Deputy Manager Dina Contella. “You really helped make us successful.”
She noted that collaboration extended well beyond the crew in orbit, with teams across the program matching that dedication throughout the expedition.
Contella shared that Expedition 73 included six cargo missions, the inaugural flight of JAXA’s (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) HTV-X1 cargo spacecraft, and more than 37,000 pounds of supplies, equipment, and scientific investigations delivered to the space station.
She also thanked the Commercial Crew Program and Flight Operations teams for helping safely transport crews to and from the station and support mission operations.
More than 1,000 employees representing 40 teams received NASA Group Achievement Awards, while 23 individuals were honored with Superior Achievement Awards for their contributions to the mission.
NASA astronaut Zena Cardman and JAXA astronaut Kimiya Yui received NASA Exceptional Bravery Honors for demonstrating exceptional courage, leadership, and composure during a medical event.
Flight Operations Director Norm Knight thanked the teams that supported Expedition 73 from the ground while recognizing the crews’ contributions throughout their missions. “You represent an astronaut corps that is the best of the best, epitomizing courage and teamwork,” Knight told the crew. “Every opportunity or challenge that came your way, you met with confidence and creativity.”
Crew members reflected on the station’s legacy as a platform for discovery, innovation, and international partnership after more than25 years of continuous human presence in orbit.
Research conducted during Expedition 73 included investigations in human physiology, biology, materials science, pharmaceutical development, and technologies designed to benefit life on Earth and future exploration missions.
The crews also discussed research aboard the station that will help prepare NASA for future missions to the Moon and Mars, including advanced life-support systems and water recovery technologies.
Beyond science and operations, the crew built strong bonds during their months in orbit. They marked birthdays, holidays, and mission milestones together, often creating elaborate cakes from the limited ingredients available aboard the station.
Many crew members said their strongest memories centered on the people around them, and that trust and teamwork remained essential to mission success.
Viewing Earth from orbit provided the crew with a powerful reminder of humanity’s shared connection.
“When you look back at Earth, what we have in common is so much more important than what makes us different,” said McClain. “We’re all on this one planet. We’re all on the same
